note: Rainforth, Foster, Ivanova, Bickford Smith, 'Modern Bayesian Experimental Design' (Statistical Science; arXiv:2302.14545v2, 2023). A modern REVIEW of Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) built explicitly around the information-theoretic EIG (Expected Information Gain) criterion: EIG(xi) = E_p(y|xi)[H[p(theta)] - H[p(theta|y,xi)]] (Eq. 1-2), equivalently the mutual information between outcome y and target theta given design xi. It reviews EIG ESTIMATION (Rao-Blackwellized for discrete y, nested Monte Carlo, multi-level Monte Carlo debiasing / Goda et al. 2022 unbiased, variational lower/upper bounds, implicit-likelihood estimators) and EIG OPTIMIZATION (black-box wrappers, stochastic-gradient schemes, and amortized policy-based methods DAD/iDAD/RL). Two manuscript claims checked. C038 (L78, EIG normative criterion) = ACCURATE: the manuscript's objective d* = argmax E[H(theta) - H(theta|y,d)] is exactly rainforth's Eq. 1-2 formalism (design d = rainforth's xi), and rainforth frames BED as choosing designs 'optimally' by information-theoretic principles. The manuscript's next sentence attributing EIG approximation to variational / amortized-policy / RL maps precisely onto the families rainforth reviews. C039 (L94, 'classical') = IMPRECISE-OK: 'classical, with a long history' correctly describes BED-the-field (rainforth itself traces BED to Lindley 1956, Shannon 1948, Fisher, Smith 1918), and rainforth is a legitimate review documenting that history; the only tension is that rainforth2024 is explicitly the MODERN companion (title 'Modern Bayesian Experimental Design'), so the 'classical' weight properly rests on chaloner1995 while rainforth supports 'long history + modern treatment'. Non-blocking.

C038.claim_text: Bayesian Experimental Design~\citep{chaloner1995bayesian,rainforth2024modern} provides a normative criterion for the information objective of the search policy $\pi$: select $d^*$ maximizing Expected Information Gain (EIG), $d^* = \arg\max_{d} \mathrm{EIG}(d) = \arg\max_{d} \mathbb{E}[H(\theta) - H(\theta \mid y, d)]$.
evidence: (a) rainforth IS a modern BED review providing the normative EIG criterion -- CONFIRMED. Abstract (lines 9-11): 'Bayesian experimental design (BED) provides a powerful and general framework for optimizing the design of experiments.' Intro (lines 48-53): 'we want to choose designs that maximize the amount of information gathered from our experiments. This desire can be formalized using Bayesian experimental design (BED) ... which provides a powerful model-based framework for choosing designs ‘optimally’ using information-theoretic principles.' Section 2.1 defines the objective and states (lines 109-113): 'As y is unknown, we cannot optimize this directly and instead target the design that maximizes the expected information gain (EIG) ... in theta'. Section 2.1 (lines 147-149): 'Our focus ... will be on maximizing the EIG defined in (2), as this is the most commonly used, and typically best-performing, approach.' => the manuscript's 'normative criterion ... select d* maximizing EIG' is exactly the paper's central objective.

(b) The manuscript's formula matches rainforth's definition EXACTLY. Manuscript Eq: EIG(d) = E[H(theta) - H(theta|y,d)]. rainforth Eq. (1): InfoGain_theta(xi,y) := H[p(theta)] - H[p(theta|y,xi)]; Eq. (2): EIG_theta(xi) := E_p(y|xi)[InfoGain_theta(xi,y)] (Section 2.1, lines 104-113). Design 'd' in the manuscript = design 'xi' in rainforth; outcome y and target theta identical. rainforth adds (line 120) that this EIG is 'the mutual information between y and theta given xi ... or the expected Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence from the posterior to the prior' -- consistent with the manuscript's 'information objective'.

(c) Corroboration from the manuscript's next sentence (L79): 'EIG can be approximated via variational methods~\citep{foster2019variational}, amortized policies~\citep{foster2021dad}, and RL~\citep{blau2022rl}.' These three families are precisely what rainforth reviews and cites as its own refs: variational bounds (Section 3.3.1, foster2019variational = rainforth [43]); amortized policy-based Deep Adaptive Design (Section 4.1, foster2021dad = rainforth [42]); and RL-based policy learning (Section 4.2, blau2022rl = rainforth [13]). This independently confirms rainforth is the correct modern-review anchor for the EIG estimation/optimization landscape the manuscript invokes.

C039.claim_text: The POMDP framework~\citep{kaelbling1998pomdp} and BED~\citep{chaloner1995bayesian,rainforth2024modern} are classical, with a long history of Bayesian-optimal experiment selection in automated science---most directly the Robot Scientist's active-learning policy~\citep{sparkes2010robot}; we connect POMDP (structure) to BED (normative policy) for the LLM-agent setting rather than claiming priority for the idea.
evidence: The adjective 'classical, with a long history' attaches to the FIELDS (POMDP and BED), not to the citing papers individually; as a statement about BED-the-method it is well-supported by rainforth itself. rainforth Section 2.1 grounds the EIG in Lindley 1956 ([95] 'On a measure of the information provided by an experiment', Annals of Math. Stat.) and Lindley 1972 ([96]), with information-theoretic roots in Shannon 1948 ([146]); its Section 2.3 traces the classical/frequentist experimental-design lineage to Fisher 1926/1936 ([39,40]) and Smith 1918 ([151]). So BED demonstrably has 'a long history', and rainforth is a legitimate review documenting it.

IMPRECISION (why not fully ACCURATE): rainforth2024modern is explicitly the MODERN companion, not itself a 'classical' work -- its title is literally 'Modern Bayesian Experimental Design' and its thesis (Abstract lines 9-11; lines 30-36) is that 'recent advances have transformed our ability' to use BED, 'outlining how the field has transformed since the publication of previous reviews [23,136]'. The 'previous reviews' it supersedes are Chaloner & Verdinelli 1995 ([23]) and Ryan et al. 2016 ([136]). Thus within the pair, the 'classical' weight properly rests on chaloner1995bayesian (the 1995 Statistical Science review), while rainforth2024 supports 'long history + modern treatment'. Bundling the modern review under the single label 'classical' is a minor emphasis mismatch, not a factual/attribution error -- the citation is defensible and non-blocking.

No misattribution: neither citation is credited with content it lacks. rainforth genuinely covers Bayesian-optimal experiment selection and its history; the manuscript's contrast 'rather than claiming priority for the idea' is exactly consistent with rainforth's own framing that BED/BAD has decades of prior work.
C039.fix: Optional (non-blocking): if maximally precise, phrase so the 'classical' descriptor rests on chaloner1995bayesian (+ foundational Lindley/Fisher work) and cite rainforth2024modern as the MODERN review of the same lineage, e.g. 'BED is classical (\citealp{chaloner1995bayesian}; see \citealp{rainforth2024modern} for a modern review)'. Substance of the sentence is correct as written.

reverse_findings.fact: rainforth's central thesis is that BED has been TRANSFORMED by modern computational advances (nested/multi-level Monte Carlo debiasing, variational and functional approximations, amortized policy-based methods such as DAD/iDAD, and RL) -- i.e. it is a 'Modern' review emphasizing recent progress, not a settled classical account. It explicitly positions itself as superseding the previous reviews Chaloner & Verdinelli 1995 [23] and Ryan et al. 2016 [136].
reverse_findings.loc: Title; Abstract lines 9-11; Introduction lines 30-36; Sections 3-4
reverse_findings.mismatch: Bears only on manuscript L94's 'classical' label (C039): rainforth is the modern companion, so lumping it under 'classical' understates its actual content. Emphasis mismatch, not a factual error. Fully consistent with C038.

reverse_findings.fact: rainforth reviews EIG ESTIMATION and OPTIMIZATION via exactly the modern families the manuscript names: variational bounds (Section 3.3.1, incl. foster2019variational=[43]), amortized policy networks / Deep Adaptive Design (Section 4.1, foster2021dad=[42], iDAD=[73]), and reinforcement-learning policy learning (Section 4.2, blau2022rl=[13]). It also covers nested Monte Carlo (Section 3.1) and unbiased multi-level Monte Carlo (Section 3.2, Goda et al. 2022 [55]).
reverse_findings.loc: Section 3.1 (nested MC); Section 3.2 (MLMC debiasing); Section 3.3.1 (variational bounds); Section 3.4.1 (stochastic gradients); Sections 4.1-4.2 (DAD/iDAD/RL policies)
reverse_findings.mismatch: None -- directly CORROBORATES manuscript C038 and the following sentence (L79: 'variational methods ... amortized policies ... and RL'). rainforth cites the same foster2019 / foster2021dad / blau2022rl works as its own [43]/[42]/[13] in these exact roles.

reverse_findings.fact: rainforth stresses that EIG-based BED, while the normative objective, is 'not without its shortfalls': it is doubly-intractable / computationally challenging (Section 3), model-dependent, and can suffer 'serious pathologies in practice if our model is misspecified' (Section 5.3); the traditional greedy per-step EIG optimization (BAD) is 'subtly sub-optimal' because myopic (Section 4).
reverse_findings.mismatch: None with C038, which cites BED only for the NORMATIVE criterion (the shortfalls rainforth notes are computational/practical, not normative). Consistent with the manuscript's separate observation (L79) that current LLM systems 'maintain no explicit posterior or stated EIG objective'. Advisory only: any manuscript passage presenting BED/EIG as practically turnkey would overstate rainforth.
